content_text:
After the Supreme Court ended race-conscious affirmative action in 2023, "colorblind" policies are being promoted as the fairest way to make decisions in schools and hiring. But equal treatment on paper doesn't always lead to equal opportunity.

A study by IPR's Chika Okafor, PhD, JD shows that even when employers ignore race and workers have the same qualifications, racial gaps still appear through people's social networks. Smaller minority groups have fewer connections who can refer them for jobs, leading to fewer opportunities and lower wages.

Okafor calls this social network discrimination, and his findings show that colorblind approaches alone don't create equal opportunity. Policies and hiring practices must account for network-based barriers to achieve true fairness.

Read the policy brief: http://spr.ly/60447Ww1k
authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

content_text:
Northwestern University continues to be a proving ground for bold climate solutions, from carbon capture and next-generation materials to environmental health, resilience, and community-centered research.

This year, Crain's Chicago Business Notable Leaders in Sustainability recognized 56 changemakers driving a cleaner, more equitable future across academia, industry, and government.

The Paula M. Trienens Institute for Sustainability and Energy is proud to celebrate the three Northwestern researchers honored in this year's cohort: R&D Pillar Capture Co-Chair Omar Farha, Adapt Initiative Co-Chair Daniel E Horton, and Trienens Institute Director Ted Sargent.

Their work spans:
🔹 Cutting-edge carbon capture and advanced materials
🔹 Climate modeling, environmental health, and community resilience
🔹 Strategic partnerships accelerating innovation and commercialization

Together, their leadership embodies the Trienens Institute's mission to advance transformative sustainability solutions.
authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

Applications are open for the Northwestern University Sustainability & Energy Fellows!

A prestigious three-year postdoctoral fellowship at the nexus of energy science and policy, technology innovation and translation, and societal impact. Applications are now open through January 30, 2026.

authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

content_text:
Nearly five years after the 2020 presidential election, millions of Americans still doubt its results—despite extensive fact-checking and court rulings showing no evidence of fraud. During a visit to Northwestern, IPR Visiting Scholar Brendan Nyhan explained that correcting falsehoods isn't enough if people never see the facts in the first place.

Nyhan's research reveals that misinformation often persists not because of partisan echo chambers, but because many Americans simply aren't paying attention—a challenge, he warns, that could threaten democratic stability as the 2028 presidential race approaches.

Read the full story: http://spr.ly/60417itAW
authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

authors: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University

content_text:
Especially in the current moment, many assume colorblind policies guarantee merit-based outcomes. My research proves they don't.

My new policy brief through the Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University reveals why: even when everyone starts with identical qualifications and employers are completely race-blind, minority workers still face systematic disadvantages. Not because of bias—because of math.

I call this "social network discrimination"—a phenomenon in which minorities suffer disadvantages in economic and social opportunities simply because their social group is smaller. I originated this concept in recent peer-reviewed research published in the Journal of Law and Economics.

This isn't just about hiring. Wherever social networks influence opportunity—education, housing, professional advancement—the same invisible mechanism operates.

The diagnosis is clear. But what's the treatment?

For policymakers: Stop assuming colorblindness equals fairness. Consider social network discrimination when shaping civil rights policies and enforcement.

For organizations: Audit your referral systems. Are they amplifying network advantages for already-connected groups?

For all of us: In settings shaped by who you know, we must build systems that counteract invisible forms of injustice—not pretend they don't exist. Let's continue that work today.

Read the full brief: https://lnkd.in/g_FRezWy
mentions: Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University
Northwestern University Pritzker School of Law

content_text:
Can a game make disagreement productive? 🎲

Northwestern University - Kellogg School of Management's Steven Franconeri developed Point Taken, a game that turns heated debates into civil conversations using visual argument mapping. "Disagreement is good. Progress isn't possible without it," he said. http://spr.ly/60487V8mM
